title: The Uncrossing Partial Order on Matchings is Eulerian
overall_summary: The paper proves that the augmented uncrossing poset hat P_n of perfect matchings of 2n cyclically ordered boundary points, ordered by resolving crossings of lensless strand diagrams and with a new bottom element adjoined, is Eulerian. The proof uses an embedding of P_n into the dual of an induced subposet of the affine symmetric-group Bruhat order, translating local adjacent-strand operations into simple-reflection descent manipulations. The main combinatorial mechanism is to choose adjacent boundary labels i,i+1, classify their strands as noncrossing, crossing, or matched, and then use the operation sigma -> s_i·sigma either as a parity-reversing involution on intervals or as part of an inductive interval decomposition. The paper also records useful facts about medial graphs with lenses: lensless reductions are well-defined, and resolving arbitrary crossings in a graph with lenses can only move downward in the uncrossing order. For the original CW complex built from crossing matchings and local resolutions, the most relevant contributions are the Eulerian signed-rank cancellation, the adjacent-toggle involutions, and the lens/loop handling needed when resolutions create non-lensless diagrams or closed components.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 setup: the uncrossing poset P_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n. Let P_n be the set of perfect matchings of the cyclically ordered boundary points 1,2,...,2n on a circle. A matching tau in P_n may be represented by a medial graph or strand diagram; such a diagram is lensless if any two strands intersect at most once. For tau in P_n, let c(tau) be the number of crossings in any lensless medial graph representing tau. Define a cover relation tau' ⋖ tau if there is a lensless medial graph G representing tau such that resolving one crossing of G gives a lensless medial graph G' representing tau'. The partial order P_n is the transitive closure of these cover relations. The paper states that P_n is graded with rank function c, has a unique maximum element, and has Catalan number C_n minimal elements. Let hat P_n be obtained from P_n by adjoining a new minimum element hat0 with c(hat0) = -1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are setup facts stated in the introduction, with references to alternative descriptions in [ALT, Ken, Lam]. The cover relation is designed so that resolving one crossing lowers c by one, and the cited earlier work supplies well-definedness and the graded-poset structure. The unique maximum and Catalan many minima are also stated as known structural properties of this uncrossing poset.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a medial graph or strand diagram on the 2n boundary points, possibly with lenses, where a lens means a pair of strands that intersect two or more times. Suppose G' and G'' are two lensless medial graphs obtained from G by repeatedly applying the three local moves displayed in the paper: the Yang-Baxter move, lens removal, and loop removal. If G' and G'' are interpreted as lensless representatives of matchings in P_n, then they represent the same matching tau in P_n. Consequently, the matching represented by a medial graph with lenses is well-defined by reducing it to any lensless graph using these moves.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper gives only a short justification: the result follows from the interpretation of medial graphs in terms of circular planar electrical networks, as developed in [ALT, Ken, Lam]. The role of the lemma is to ensure that although a graph with lenses may have many possible reduction sequences using Yang-Baxter, lens-removal, and loop-removal moves, all lensless outcomes define the same element of P_n. This well-definedness is later used when resolving crossings produces diagrams that are not initially lensless or that contain removable closed loops.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a medial graph, possibly with lenses, that represents a matching tau in P_n in the sense of Lemma 1. Let G' be obtained from G by resolving any number of crossings, in arbitrary choices of the two local resolutions, and by removing all closed interior loops that result. If G' represents a matching tau' in P_n, then tau >= tau' in the uncrossing partial order P_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof translates the operation to circular planar electrical networks. In the language of [Lam], G is the medial graph of an electrical network Gamma with response point L(Gamma) lying in the electroid stratum E_tau. Resolving a crossing in the medial graph corresponds to either deleting an edge of Gamma or contracting an edge of Gamma; closed interior loops correspond to isolated interior components after these operations and can be discarded. Thus G' is the medial graph of a network Gamma' obtained from Gamma by edge deletions and contractions. The closure relation for electroid strata, cited as [Lam, Theorem 5.7], says that the closure of E_tau is the disjoint union of E_eta over eta <= tau. Therefore the stratum of Gamma', namely E_tau', must satisfy tau' <= tau.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tilde S_{2n} be the affine symmetric group with Bruhat order, and let s_i be the simple reflection indexed by i in Z/2nZ. For f in tilde S_{2n}, define the left descent set D_L(f) = {i in Z/2nZ : s_i f < f} and the right descent set D_R(f) = {i in Z/2nZ : f s_i < f}. If f <= g in Bruhat order and i belongs to D_L(g) but not D_L(f), then f <= s_i g and s_i f <= g. If f <= g and i belongs to D_R(g) but not D_R(f), then f <= g s_i and f s_i <= g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is the standard lifting property for Bruhat order, cited from Björner--Brenti, Proposition 2.2.7. The proof is not reproduced in the paper. Its use here is to turn a descent-set difference between the affine permutations corresponding to two matchings into two new Bruhat inequalities, which then translate back into inequalities in the uncrossing poset because P_n is embedded as the dual of an induced Bruhat subposet.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Adjacent-strand classification and the operation s_i·tau
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For tau in P_n and i in Z/2nZ, classify the two strands beginning at adjacent boundary labels i and i+1 as follows: i is in A(tau) if the strands labeled i and i+1 do not cross; i is in B(tau) if those strands cross; and i is in C(tau) if i is matched with i+1, so the two boundary labels lie on the same strand. These three sets A(tau), B(tau), C(tau) form a disjoint union of Z/2nZ. Let iota: P_n -> tilde S_{2n} be Lam's injection into the affine symmetric group, write g_tau = iota(tau), and define s_i·tau by g_{s_i·tau} = s_i g_tau s_i. Then i in A(tau) exactly when s_i g_tau s_i < g_tau, equivalently s_i·tau covers tau in P_n; i in B(tau) exactly when s_i g_tau s_i > g_tau, equivalently s_i·tau is covered by tau; and i in C(tau) exactly when s_i g_tau s_i = g_tau, equivalently s_i·tau = tau.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a key package of definitions and equivalences stated before Lemma 4. The paper explains the geometric meaning in one direction: if i lies in A(tau), then s_i·tau is obtained by adding a crossing between the strands labeled i and i+1 close to the boundary at i and i+1. The inequalities involving g_tau come from the embedding of P_n into the dual Bruhat order; adding a crossing in P_n corresponds to moving downward in Bruhat order for the associated affine permutation, while removing a crossing corresponds to moving upward in Bruhat order.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tau <= eta in P_n. Let i in Z/2nZ. If i belongs to A(tau) and to B(eta), meaning the adjacent strands i,i+1 do not cross in tau but do cross in eta, then tau <= s_i·eta and s_i·tau <= eta in P_n, where s_i·rho is defined by g_{s_i·rho} = s_i g_rho s_i under Lam's affine-Bruhat embedding.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because the embedding iota realizes P_n as dual to an induced Bruhat subposet, tau <= eta in P_n translates to g_eta <= g_tau in affine Bruhat order. The condition i in A(tau) implies i lies in both D_L(g_tau) and D_R(g_tau). The condition i in B(eta) implies i lies in neither D_L(g_eta) nor D_R(g_eta). Apply the Bruhat lifting property of Lemma 3 to f = g_eta and g = g_tau using the left descent difference: this gives s_i g_eta <= g_tau. Since g_{s_i·eta} = s_i g_eta s_i is greater than s_i g_eta in Bruhat order, i is not a right descent of s_i g_eta. Applying Lemma 3 again on the right gives s_i g_eta s_i <= g_tau, i.e. g_{s_i·eta} <= g_tau, which is equivalent by duality to tau <= s_i·eta. The proof of s_i·tau <= eta is obtained by the symmetric argument interchanging left and right descent sets.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let tau <= eta in P_n. If i belongs to A(tau), meaning the strands beginning at adjacent boundary labels i and i+1 are distinct and do not cross in tau, then i does not belong to C(eta); equivalently, eta cannot match i directly with i+1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper says this follows easily from the uncrossing definition. The underlying point is that in a downward sequence of crossing resolutions from eta to tau, the configuration in which the adjacent boundary labels i and i+1 form one strand in eta cannot become, at the lower element tau, two distinct adjacent strands that do not cross. This lemma is used to rule out fixed points of the operation sigma -> s_i·sigma when constructing parity-reversing involutions on intervals.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P_n be the uncrossing poset of matchings of 2n cyclically ordered boundary points, ranked by c(tau), the number of crossings in a lensless medial graph. Let hat P_n be P_n with a new minimum element hat0 adjoined and ranked by c(hat0) = -1. A finite graded poset with unique minimum and maximum is Eulerian if, for every interval [x,y] with x < y, the number of elements of even rank in [x,y] equals the number of elements of odd rank in [x,y]. Then hat P_n is Eulerian.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For any subset S of hat P_n, write chi(S) = sum_{rho in S} (-1)^{c(rho)}. It suffices to prove chi([tau,eta]) = 0 for every proper interval. First consider intervals tau < eta entirely inside P_n. The proof is by descending induction on c(tau)+c(eta), with rank-one intervals and the top-base case clear. Since tau is not maximal, A(tau) is nonempty; choose i in A(tau). Lemma 5 rules out i in C(eta), leaving two cases. Case 1: i in A(eta). Then s_i·eta covers eta. One has [tau,eta] = [tau,s_i·eta] minus T, where T = {sigma : tau <= sigma <= s_i·eta and sigma not <= eta}. The key claim is T = {sigma : s_i·tau <= sigma <= s_i·eta and sigma not <= eta}. To prove it, take sigma in T. If i in A(sigma), then since i in B(s_i·eta), Lemma 4 applied to sigma < s_i·eta gives sigma <= eta, contradiction. If i in C(sigma), this contradicts Lemma 5 applied to tau <= sigma. Therefore i in B(sigma), and Lemma 4 applied to tau <= sigma gives s_i·tau <= sigma. The reverse inclusion is immediate. Hence T = [s_i·tau,s_i·eta] minus [s_i·tau,eta]. By the induction hypothesis, the signed chi of each interval appearing in this difference, and also of [tau,s_i·eta], is zero; hence chi([tau,eta]) = 0. Case 2: i in B(eta). For every sigma in [tau,eta], Lemma 5 rules out i in C(sigma). If i in A(sigma), Lemma 4 applied to sigma <= eta shows s_i·sigma <= eta, and tau <= sigma <= s_i·sigma gives s_i·sigma in the interval. If i in B(sigma), Lemma 4 applied to tau <= sigma shows tau <= s_i·sigma, and s_i·sigma <= sigma <= eta. Thus sigma -> s_i·sigma is a well-defined involution of [tau,eta] with no fixed points, and it changes c by 1, so it cancels even and odd ranks. Case 3, i in C(eta), is impossible by Lemma 5. This proves all proper intervals inside P_n. Now consider intervals [hat0,eta]. The case c(eta)=0 is immediate, so assume c(eta) >= 1 and choose i in B(eta), which is nonempty. The same adjacent-toggle argument pairs, with opposite parity, all nonzero sigma <= eta for which i is not in C(sigma); the remaining set is S = {hat0} union {sigma in P_n : sigma < eta and i in C(sigma)}. The paper then proves S has a unique maximal element kappa. Choose a medial graph G for eta such that the strands starting at i and i+1 cross at a point q before either meets any other strand. Resolve q in the direction that matches i directly with i+1, and let kappa be the matching represented by the resulting graph G'. For any sigma in S, a lensless graph G'' for sigma can be obtained from G by uncrossing a subset of crossings. Since i is in C(sigma), q must be among the uncrossed crossings. If q is resolved in G'' in the same direction as in G', then G'' is obtained from G' by further uncrossings, so Lemma 2 gives sigma <= kappa. If q is resolved in the other direction, changing that local resolution to the G' direction produces a graph representing the same sigma after a closed interior loop is removed; Lemma 2 again gives sigma <= kappa. Thus S = [hat0,kappa]. By induction chi(S)=0, and together with the parity cancellation outside S this gives chi([hat0,eta])=0. Therefore every proper interval in hat P_n has equal numbers of even- and odd-rank elements.

proof_techniques.name: Translate uncrossing questions to affine Bruhat lifting
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Lam's embedding iota: P_n -> tilde S_{2n}, writing g_tau = iota(tau), which realizes P_n as dual to an induced subposet of affine Bruhat order. Local operations on adjacent boundary strands are encoded by conjugation g_tau -> s_i g_tau s_i. Whether this operation adds a crossing, removes a crossing, or fixes the matching is detected by the Bruhat comparison between s_i g_tau s_i and g_tau, equivalently by the left and right descent sets of g_tau. The standard Bruhat lifting property then gives inequalities between matchings after toggling an adjacent pair.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the matchings allowed by the word w can be embedded into, or compared with, the full uncrossing poset P_n, Bruhat-style lifting may supply order inequalities for local resolutions. Even without a literal affine-Bruhat embedding, the pattern suggests proving analogues of Lemma 4 for adjacent endpoints or for local word-compatible pairs.

proof_techniques.name: Parity-reversing adjacent-toggle involution
proof_techniques.description: When an interval [tau,eta] has an adjacent index i such that the i,i+1 strands do not cross in tau but do cross in eta, Lemma 4 shows that the operation sigma -> s_i·sigma stays inside the interval. Lemma 5 eliminates the fixed case i in C(sigma), so the map is a fixed-point-free involution. Since s_i·sigma differs from sigma by adding or removing exactly one crossing, it reverses the parity of the rank c and forces the signed rank sum of the interval to vanish.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For F_w, links or face posets may have signed Euler characteristic controlled by pairing cells that differ by a local crossing toggle. A solver could look for a word-compatible version of the A/B/C trichotomy and prove that the toggle preserves the set of admissible quasi-reduced cancellation matchings.

proof_techniques.name: Inductive interval decomposition by adding a crossing to the top
proof_techniques.description: When the chosen adjacent index i lies in A(tau) and also A(eta), the paper enlarges the interval [tau,eta] to [tau,s_i·eta], where s_i·eta is obtained by adding a crossing near the boundary. The original interval is the enlarged interval minus the elements not below eta. A key claim identifies that exceptional set as [s_i·tau,s_i·eta] minus [s_i·tau,eta], using Lemmas 4 and 5. Since all these new intervals have larger c(lower)+c(upper), descending induction applies to their signed rank sums.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a template for recursive proofs about resolution complexes: enlarge a cell-poset interval by allowing one extra local crossing, identify the complement by forcing the corresponding lower endpoint also to be toggled, and use induction. It may help analyze stars or links in F_w if adding a crossing preserves admissibility or can be confined to a controlled subcomplex.

proof_techniques.name: Residual subset with a unique maximum for intervals from the adjoined bottom
proof_techniques.description: For intervals [hat0,eta], a parity-reversing toggle cancels most nonzero elements. The uncancelled residual consists of hat0 together with matchings sigma < eta in which a chosen adjacent pair i,i+1 is directly matched. The paper constructs a unique maximal residual element kappa by taking a medial graph for eta, resolving the first crossing q between the i and i+1 strands in the direction that directly matches i to i+1, and then showing every residual sigma lies below kappa. The proof uses the fact that any sigma below eta can be represented by uncrossing a subset of crossings of eta, plus Lemma 2 to handle the possibility that resolving q in the other direction creates a removable closed loop.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This resembles a coning or recursive collapse argument: after pairing most cells, the unpaired cells form a smaller interval with a cone point or unique maximal element. For F_w, one could try to choose a cancellable adjacent inverse pair in the word and show the subcomplex of matchings that pair it directly has a unique maximal or contractible core.

proof_techniques.name: Lens and loop normalization
proof_techniques.description: The paper explicitly allows medial graphs with lenses during intermediate operations. Lemma 1 says reducing such a graph to lensless form by Yang-Baxter, lens-removal, and loop-removal moves gives a well-defined matching. Lemma 2 says that resolving crossings in any order and removing closed loops always moves downward in the uncrossing order, even if the intermediate graph has lenses. This lets the proof use convenient non-lensless diagrams without losing control of the poset relation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original definition of F_w permits embedded intervals with multiple pairwise intersections, so resolving one crossing can interact with lenses or create closed components in intermediate pictures. These lemmas suggest proving a normalization theorem ensuring that isotopy classes and face attachments are independent of lens-removal choices.

proof_techniques.name: Signed-rank Euler characteristic instead of explicit topology
proof_techniques.description: Eulerianity is proved by showing chi([x,y]) = sum (-1)^{rank} = 0 for every proper interval, not by constructing spheres or shellings. The argument repeatedly decomposes finite sets of matchings into parity-cancelling pairs plus smaller intervals. This gives a purely combinatorial route to the Eulerian property of the face-like poset.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Contractibility of F_w is stronger than an Euler characteristic computation, but signed-rank cancellation can still test whether proposed local links have the Euler characteristic of spheres or balls. It may also guide discrete Morse matchings on the cells of F_w.

proof_techniques.name: Use of first crossing near the boundary
proof_techniques.description: In the proof for intervals involving hat0, after choosing i in B(eta), the paper chooses a representative medial graph in which the strands starting at i and i+1 cross at a point q before either strand meets any other strand. Resolving this first crossing in the appropriate direction forces i and i+1 to become directly matched and defines the maximal residual element kappa. The choice of a first boundary crossing isolates the local operation from the rest of the diagram.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a word-labeled matching, isolating the first interaction of two boundary arcs may allow one to perform a local cancellation or contraction while preserving the label constraints. This is especially relevant when attempting induction on the number of letters or crossings.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that P_n has a topological interpretation as the closure poset of electroid cells E_tau in a compactification E_n of the space of circular planar electrical networks, with closure relation overline{E_tau} = union_{tau' <= tau} E_tau'. It also notes that the same partial order appears in the positive orthogonal Grassmannian and ABJM scattering-amplitude literature. The introduction states that [ALT] proves P_n is thin, meaning intervals of length two are diamonds; this is relevant because thin plus shellable would imply strong sphere-like interval topology. The paper conjectures that hat P_n is lexicographically shellable and gives an explicit EL-shelling only for hat P_3; shellability is not proved in this paper. A key external input used in the final proof is a comment after [Lam, Lemma 4.11]: if sigma <= eta, then a lensless medial graph representing sigma can be obtained from a graph representing eta by uncrossing a subset of its crossings. In the last paragraph of the printed proof, the displayed conditions involving C(eta) appear to be typographical: since i was chosen in B(eta), the involution and residual set must be separated according to whether i lies in C(sigma) for the varying element sigma.
why_relevant: Directly studies matchings of 2n boundary points ordered by resolving crossings. Its Eulerian and rank arguments are central for understanding any CW complex whose cells are built from crossing matchings and uncrossing maps.
